channel.title: quant-ph (TEST FEED)
channel.description: Local test feed. Fictional papers only.

item.title: [TEST] High-threshold decoding of biased-noise stabilizer codes
item.description: arXiv:2606.99001 Announce Type: new Abstract: We present a decoding scheme for stabilizer codes under biased Pauli noise. Exploiting the asymmetry between X- and Z-type errors, our decoder achieves a higher threshold than standard minimum-weight perfect matching and reduces logical error rates across a range of code distances. We provide analytic bounds on the threshold and benchmark performance on the surface code and color code under circuit-level noise.
item.dc:creator: Alice Researcher, Bob Collaborator

item.title: [TEST] Low-overhead magic state preparation via code switching
item.description: arXiv:2606.99002 Announce Type: new Abstract: We propose protocols for preparing high-fidelity magic states with reduced overhead by combining code switching between a triorthogonal code and a CSS code with lattice surgery operations. Our resource estimation shows a factor-of-three reduction in the number of physical qubits required for logical T-gate injection compared to standard distillation methods, while maintaining the same output fidelity under circuit-level noise.
item.dc:creator: Carol Faulttolerant

item.title: [TEST] Near-optimal quantum algorithms for sparse linear systems via qubitization
item.description: arXiv:2606.99003 Announce Type: new Abstract: We present quantum algorithms for solving sparse linear systems that achieve near-optimal query complexity through qubitization and quantum signal processing. Our approach improves the dependence on the condition number compared to the original HHL algorithm and extends naturally to solving differential equations. We also give a matching quantum lower bound via a reduction from parity, demonstrating tightness of the construction.
item.dc:creator: Dan Algorithm, Eve Speedup

item.title: [TEST] Separations between BQP and the polynomial hierarchy under structured oracles
item.description: arXiv:2606.99004 Announce Type: new Abstract: We construct a structured oracle relative to which BQP is not contained in the polynomial hierarchy, strengthening previous separations that relied on random oracles. Our construction exploits properties of the forrelation problem and yields tight query complexity lower bounds. As a corollary we obtain new evidence that QMA is strictly more powerful than BQP under plausible circuit complexity assumptions.
item.dc:creator: Frank Complexity

item.title: [TEST] Escaping barren plateaus in parameterized circuits via layer-wise training
item.description: arXiv:2606.99005 Announce Type: new Abstract: Trainability of deep parameterized circuits is hindered by exponentially vanishing gradients. We propose a layer-wise training strategy combined with identity-block initialization that provably avoids this phenomenon for circuits of polynomial depth. Numerical experiments on the variational eigensolver and quantum approximate optimization tasks on noisy devices confirm faster convergence and higher final fidelity compared to global random initialization.
item.dc:creator: Grace NISQ, Hank Variational

item.title: [TEST] Tight Trotter error bounds for time-dependent Hamiltonians with applications to quantum chemistry
item.description: arXiv:2606.99006 Announce Type: new Abstract: We derive tight commutator-based error bounds for higher-order product formula simulation of time-dependent Hamiltonians. Applied to electronic structure problems, our bounds imply a polynomial reduction in circuit depth for first-principles energy estimation compared to prior LCU-based methods. We also demonstrate improved scaling for simulation of lattice models relevant to correlated materials.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Simulator

item.title: [TEST] Provable advantages of quantum kernels for learning periodic functions
item.description: arXiv:2606.99007 Announce Type: new Abstract: We establish a provable learning-theoretic separation between quantum and classical kernel methods for a class of periodic Boolean functions. Our quantum feature map encodes data into a superposition of Fourier modes and achieves optimal sample complexity while the best classical kernel requires exponentially more samples. We also derive dequantization bounds showing the separation is robust to small perturbations of the input distribution.
item.dc:creator: Jane QML, Karl Learning

item.title: [TEST] One-shot bounds on entanglement-assisted capacity via smooth entropies
item.description: arXiv:2606.99008 Announce Type: new Abstract: We derive tight one-shot achievability and converse bounds for entanglement-assisted classical communication over a quantum channel using smooth min- and max-entropies. Our results recover the quantum Shannon-theoretic formula in the i.i.d. limit and yield improved finite-blocklength rates. We also characterize the tradeoff between entanglement cost and communication rate through a resource-theoretic lens.
item.dc:creator: Laura QIT

item.title: [TEST] Optimal scheduling for entanglement distribution in multi-hop quantum networks
item.description: arXiv:2606.99009 Announce Type: new Abstract: We study the problem of distributing entanglement across a chain of intermediate nodes connected by lossy optical links. By modeling the network as a queuing system with probabilistic Bell-state measurement success, we derive optimal link-layer scheduling policies that maximize end-to-end entanglement generation rates. Our protocols require only local memory and classical communication, making them directly implementable in near-term quantum repeater hardware.
item.dc:creator: Mike Network

item.title: [TEST] Noise-tolerant device-independent protocols from the entropy accumulation theorem
item.description: arXiv:2606.99010 Announce Type: new Abstract: We construct a two-party protocol for generating a shared secret key in the fully device-independent setting, where both measurement devices are untrusted. Security against general quantum adversaries is proved using the entropy accumulation theorem without any assumption on the internal workings of the devices. Our protocol tolerates detection efficiencies as low as 90% and achieves positive key rates at noise levels reachable with current photonic Bell-test experiments.
item.dc:creator: Nancy Crypto, Oscar QKD

item.title: [TEST] Verifiable blind quantum computation with a secure classical client
item.description: arXiv:2606.99017 Announce Type: new Abstract: We study verifiable blind quantum computing protocols that allow a client to securely delegate a quantum computation to an untrusted server. The protocol provides blindness of the input and computation, verifiability of the returned outcome, and a composable security proof against malicious servers, while keeping the client nearly classical.
item.dc:creator: Vera Blind, Calvin Secure

item.title: [TEST] Generic structures of quantum auction and secure summation protocols
item.description: arXiv:2606.99016 Announce Type: new Abstract: Secure multi-party computation addresses the problem of jointly computing functions of private inputs while revealing minimal information about individual data. We identify structural symmetries between quantum sealed-bid auction protocols and secure multi-party summation, showing that auction primitives such as revenue estimation and winner determination reduce to repeated invocations of a secure summation oracle. The framework is protocol-agnostic and applies to gate-based and photonic implementations.
item.dc:creator: Alice Auction, Sam Summation

item.title: [TEST] Concrete security of module lattice-based KEMs in the QROM
item.description: arXiv:2606.99011 Announce Type: new Abstract: We provide tighter security reductions for module learning-with-errors based key encapsulation mechanisms in the quantum random oracle model. Our analysis improves the concrete bit-security estimates for the NIST-standardized schemes and closes a gap between classical and quantum adversary bounds. We also show that the NTRU-based constructions achieve comparable security under a weaker hardness assumption when the modulus is chosen appropriately.
item.dc:creator: Paul PQC

item.title: [TEST] Crosstalk-suppressed two-qubit gates in a superconducting processor via pulse shaping
item.description: arXiv:2606.99012 Announce Type: new Abstract: We demonstrate pulse-shaped CZ gates on a 20-qubit transmon processor that suppress leakage to non-computational states and residual ZZ coupling between neighboring qubits. Randomized benchmarking yields an average two-qubit gate fidelity of 99.6% with simultaneous operation on all pairs, a significant improvement over standard flat-top pulses. The technique is compatible with existing control hardware and requires no additional calibration overhead.
item.dc:creator: Quinn Hardware

item.title: [TEST] Sub-Heisenberg phase estimation using adaptive measurements on GHZ states
item.description: arXiv:2606.99013 Announce Type: new Abstract: We propose an adaptive measurement scheme that exploits entangled probe states to achieve phase sensitivity scaling as N^{-1} beyond the standard quantum limit. Our protocol uses a sequence of single-qubit feedback operations informed by prior measurement outcomes, and we show via quantum Cramér-Rao bound analysis that it saturates the quantum Fisher information. Experimental implementation is discussed for Ramsey interferometry with trapped ions and optical clocks.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Sensing

item.title: [TEST] Contextuality as a resource for advantage in sequential measurement tasks
item.description: arXiv:2606.99014 Announce Type: new Abstract: We characterize the role of Kochen-Specker contextuality in sequential measurement scenarios and show that any advantage over classical strategies in a parity-oblivious communication task requires a contextual preparation procedure. Our proof uses a generalization of the Spekkens framework to weak measurements and connects contextuality to violations of macrorealism via a Leggett-Garg inequality. We also discuss implications for thermodynamic work extraction from quantum systems.
item.dc:creator: Sam Foundations

item.title: [TEST] Superconductivity and charge order in doped Mott insulators on the triangular lattice
item.description: arXiv:2606.99015 Announce Type: cross Abstract: We study the competition between superconducting and charge-density-wave order in the doped Hubbard model on the triangular lattice using determinant quantum Monte Carlo. At half filling we find a Mott insulating phase; upon hole doping a superconducting dome emerges alongside incommensurate charge order. The phase diagram is consistent with recent angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy measurements on transition-metal dichalcogenides.
item.dc:creator: Tom Condensed
